original_abstract: Silver-copper (AgCu) bimetallic catalysts hold great potential for electrochemical carbon dioxide reduction reaction (CO<sub>2</sub>RR), which is a promising way to realize the goal of carbon neutrality. Although a wide variety of AgCu catalysts have been developed so far, it is relatively less explored how these AgCu catalysts evolve during CO<sub>2</sub>RR. The absence of insights into their stability makes the dynamic catalytic sites elusive and hampers the design of AgCu catalysts in a rational manner. Here, we synthesized intermixed and phase-separated AgCu nanoparticles on carbon paper electrodes and investigated their evolution behavior in CO<sub>2</sub>RR. Our time-sequential electron microscopy and elemental mapping studies show that Cu possesses high mobility in AgCu under CO<sub>2</sub>RR conditions, which can leach out from the catalysts by migrating to the bimetallic catalyst surface, detaching from the catalysts, and agglomerating as new particles. Besides, Ag and Cu manifest a trend to phase-separate into Cu-rich and Ag-rich grains, regardless of the starting catalyst structure. The composition of the Cu-rich and Ag-rich grains diverges during the reaction and eventually approaches thermodynamic values, i.e., Ag<sub>0.88</sub>Cu<sub>0.12</sub> and Ag<sub>0.05</sub>Cu<sub>0.95</sub>. The separation between Ag and Cu has been observed in the bulk and on the surface of the catalysts, highlighting the importance of AgCu phase boundaries for CO<sub>2</sub>RR. In addition, an operando high-energy-resolution X-ray absorption spectroscopy study confirms the metallic state of Cu in AgCu as the catalytically active sites during CO<sub>2</sub>RR. Taken together, this work provides a comprehensive understanding of the chemical and structural evolution behavior of AgCu catalysts in CO<sub>2</sub>RR.
humanized_abstract: Silver-copper (AgCu) bimetallic catalysts are highly desired for the electrochemical reduction of carbon dioxide (CO<sub>2</sub>RR), which is crucial to achieving a carbon neutral future. Although a large number of catalysts with varied compositions have been fabricated in recent years, their evolutions during CO<sub>2</sub>RR are rarely understood in detail. The unknown evolutions of the catalysts hinder us from determining the catalytic active sites at different stages and further designing highly efficient catalysts in a designed fashion. In this work, we in situ grew intermixed and phase-separated AgCu nanoparticles on the surface of carbon paper electrodes and elucidated their evolutions in CO<sub>2</sub>RR through comprehensive analysis including time-sequential electron microscopy and elemental mapping. The results show that Cu exhibited higher activity than Ag in the entire carbon paper electrodes, suggesting that Cu can leach out from the bulk to the surface of the catalyst, while it can also detach from the catalysts and further precipitate as small nanoparticles. In addition, the bimetallic catalysts exhibited obvious tendency to form Cu-rich and Ag-rich grains after a short period of 1 h, regardless of the initial mixed structures or separated phases. Interestingly, with the ongoing reaction time, the compositions of the Cu-rich and Ag-rich grains at the surface showed further diversification and reached their corresponding thermodynamic values, i.e., 78.67 at. % Ag and 95.05 at. % Cu for Ag<sub>0.88</sub>Cu<sub>0.12</sub> and Ag<sub>0.05</sub>Cu<sub>0.95</sub>, respectively. Notably, the observed obvious phase separation in both the bulk and surface of the catalysts confirm that the formation of the phase boundary between the Ag and Cu components is favorable to the CO<sub>2</sub>RR process. More importantly, the operando high-energy-resolution X-ray absorption spectroscopy further confirms that Cu element maintains in the metallic state as the catalytically active sites for CO<sub>2</sub>RR. Therefore, the present study provides for the first time an in-depth understanding of the various evolutions of the AgCu catalysts during CO<sub>2</sub>RR.
